retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Anal Canal -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Arterial Supply and Lymphatic Drainage
retrieved_docs.content: Above the pectinate line, the anal canal is supplied by terminal branches of the superior rectal artery, a branch of the inferior mesenteric artery. Lymphatic fluid from this region is conveyed to the inferior mesenteric lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis, Rectum -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The lymphatic drainage of the rectum follows the arterial supply as well. The rectum drains into the superior rectal lymphatics to the retroperitoneal inferior mesenteric lymph nodes. Additionally, the middle and inferior hemorrhoidal vessels have lymphatic drainage into the internal iliac nodes. Below the dentate line in the anal canal, the lymph drains to the inguinal nodes. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Anal Canal -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Arterial Supply and Lymphatic Drainage
retrieved_docs.content: Below the pectinate line, the anal canal is served by the middle and inferior rectal arteries. The middle rectal artery is a branch of the internal iliac artery, while the inferior rectal artery is a branch of the internal pudendal artery that arises from the internal iliac artery. The lymphatic drainage below the pectinate line is primarily to the superficial inguinal lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Anal Triangle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Above the pectinate line, the superior rectal artery is responsible for blood supply. It is a branch of the inferior mesenteric artery. Venous drainage above the pectinate line drains to the portal venous system, which is critical to differentiate from below the pectinate line. The internal hemorrhoidal plexus drains to the superior rectal vein. This blood continues to drain through the inferior mesenteric vein, splenic vein, and then to the portal vein. [6] Lymphatic drainage of the anal canal above the pectinate line is to the internal iliac lymph nodes. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anal Carcinoma -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough understanding of the anatomy of the anal canal, perianal skin, and lymphatic drainage pathways is essential for the accurate diagnosis and effective management of anal carcinoma. The anal region consists of the anal canal and the perianal skin within a 5 cm radius of the anal verge. While the diagnostic evaluation for anal canal and perianal malignancies is similar, treatment strategies differ based on tumor staging and anatomical location. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Anal Canal -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: An excellent approach to understanding the arterial supply, venous drainage, and lymphatics of the anal canal is to consider the supply above and below the pectinate line and the area of overlap. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Anal Triangle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Below the pectinate line, the inferior rectal arteries and veins supply and drain the muscles and skin around the anus. The inferior rectal vessels are branches from the internal pudendal vessels. Blood from this area below the pectinate line ultimately drains to the caval venous system. Blood from the external hemorrhoidal plexus drains into the inferior rectal vein. The blood continues to drain to the internal pudendal vein, internal iliac vein, common iliac vein, and lastly, to the inferior vena cava. [6] Lymphatic drainage of the perineum below the pectinate line is to the superficial inguinal lymph nodes. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Radiation Therapy for Anal Cancer -- Introduction -- Anatomy
retrieved_docs.content: The anal canal runs from the anal verge (the visible junction between the internal anal canal and hair-bearing keratinized external anal skin) to the anorectal ring (the location in which the rectum enters the puborectalis sling). The anal canal is approximately 3 to 5 cm in length. The dentate line is the anatomic boundary where mucosa changes from non-keratinized squamous epithelium to colorectal-type columnar mucosa proximally. It divides the upper and lower anal canal. The anal margin, also referred to as perianal skin, is a rim of tissue around the anus encompassed by a 5 cm radius, and it bears true squamous epithelium. Lymphatic drainage of anal cancers depends upon its location relative to the dentate line. Cancers above the dentate line drain to the presacral and internal iliac nodes. Cancers below the dentate line drain to superficial inguinal and femoral nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Anal Canal -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: During surgery involving the anal canal, the surgeon has a variety of techniques available to identify the transition between the upper portion and lower portion of the anal canal. Identifying the region of the pectinate line is vital to understanding the vascular supply and drainage and innervation of the segments of the anal canal for surgical considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: Internal Anal Sphincterotomy -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The anal canal can be described in 2 ways, the functional (surgical) or anatomic anal canal. The surgical anal canal is about 4 cm long and extends from the anal verge to the anorectal ring or puborectalis sling. The anatomic anal canal is approximately 2 cm long and starts at the anal verge extending to the dentate line.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Anal Canal -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The tight space and compact anatomy of the anal canal contribute to a difficult compartment for surgical procedures. For instance, the anatomy of the anal canal makes sphincter-saving surgery during cases of lower rectal cancer very challenging. These surgeries have the potential for negative outcomes, such as anal sphincter compromise with incontinence or permanent colostomy. Given the limited window available for surgeons within the anal canal, other approaches have been explored, such as transvaginal, transperineal, trans-sacral, and abdominal. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Anal Triangle -- Structure and Function -- Anal Canal
retrieved_docs.content: The anal canal is the hindmost part of the gastrointestinal system. [1] There is both an anatomic and surgical definition of the anal canal. The anatomic anal canal spans from the anal verge to the pectinate (dentate) line. The surgical anal canal is longer and spans from the anal verge to the anorectal line. [2] The anal verge is the junction of anoderm with perianal skin.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis: Anal Canal -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The anal canal is typically divided into superior and inferior segments by the pectinate, or dentate, line. The pectinate line is a visible zig-zagging line formed from the inferior aspect of longitudinal folds known as the anal columns or anal valves. The pectinate line demarcates the superior two-thirds of the anal canal from the inferior one-third. It also serves as an embryologic landmark that explains the differing arterial supply, venous drainage, lymphatic drainage, and nervous supply of the segments of the anal canal.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Abdomen and Pelvis, Rectum -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), the most distal branch of the abdominal aorta before its bifurcation, ends as the superior rectal (hemorrhoidal) arteries. This artery supplies the rectum and upper third of the anal canal. The rectum receives additional vascular contributions from the middle rectal artery, which is a branch of the internal iliac artery, supplying the distal rectum and proximal anal canal. The inferior rectal artery comes off the internal pudendal artery, which is also a branch of the internal iliac artery. Collaterals between the inferior and superior hemorrhoidal arteries exist at the level of the dentate line, making the incidence of rectal ischemia low. [2] [4] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Abdominoperineal Resection -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Anal Canal, Anus, and Anal Sphincter Complex
retrieved_docs.content: The pectinate (dentate) line, an irregular ring formed by the anal valves, further divides the anal canal based on its embryological origin. This line separates the canal into upper and lower sections, each with distinct structural and neurovascular characteristics. The upper section, derived from the hindgut, receives blood from the superior rectal artery, a branch of the IMA. In contrast, the lower section, which originates from the ectoderm, is supplied by the inferior rectal artery, a branch of the pudendal artery. [5] Lymphatic drainage of the anal canal follows distinct pathways: above the pectinate line, it drains into the internal iliac lymph nodes, while below the pectinate line, it drains into the inguinal lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Rectum and Anal Canal -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Below the pectinate line, the anal canal has an ectodermal origin and is predominantly lined by stratified squamous epithelium. The inferior rectal canal obtains its blood supply from the inferior rectal artery, which originates from the internal iliac artery. Blood returns via the inferior rectal vein, which ultimately drains into the inferior vena cava. [17] The lymphatic drainage of the inferior anal canal is the superficial inguinal lymph nodes. The inferior anal canal receives somatic innervation via the branches of the pudendal nerve, specifically the inferior anal/rectal nerve. Its efferent somatic innervation controls the voluntary actions of the external anal sphincter. Due to its somatic innervation, it can sense pain, temperature, and touch. This difference in innervation is implicated in the clinical presentation of hemorrhoids and anal fissures. Patients with lesions below the pectinate line usually complain of significant pain with any contact near the lesions, and pain is often unbearable, whereas lesions above the pectinate line often go unnoticed because of its visceral innervation and the lack of pain sensation. [2]

Together, these relationships illustrate that venous structures serve as both direct and indirect sites for varicose vein interventions and that the hemorrhoidal plexus provides the anatomical basis for hemorrhoidal procedures, linking anatomical locations with their corresponding therapeutic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 238
retrieved_docs.content: venous procedures such as varicose‑vein ligation and stripping, and anorectal hemorrhoidal treatments through common procedural categories (incision, excision, occlusion), shared anatomical sites (vein, arterial and venous plexuses), and unified clinical objectives of restoring perfusion, relieving venous congestion, and managing hemorrhoidal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 246
retrieved_docs.content: for hemorrhoids are interconnected through shared categories of vessel incision, occlusion, and excision, common procedural sites (arteries, veins, internal rectal venous plexus), and therapeutic objectives such as blood drainage and vein ligation. Understanding these anatomical sites, procedural hierarchies, and associated pathologies is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic planning, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2649
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhoids is classified as a type of venous disorder (اضطراب الوريد), reflecting its origin in abnormal dilation and dysfunction of the venous vessels of the anorectal region. This relationship underscores that hemorrhoids arise from underlying vascular pathology, linking the clinical presentation of swollen, painful, or bleeding anal cushions directly to disturbances in venous blood flow and vessel integrity. Recognizing hemorrhoids as a manifestation of a broader venous disorder informs both diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies, emphasizing the importance of addressing venous health to manage and prevent hemorrhoidal disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 332
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of hemorrhoidal plexus is the anatomical site where the procedure called عملية البواسير is performed. عملية البواسير is a specific instance of hemorrhoid procedures, which constitute the broader category of interventions targeting hemorrhoidal disease. Recognizing that the Structure of hemorrhoidal plexus serves as the procedure site for عملية البواسير and that عملية البواسير belongs to hemorrhoid procedures aids clinicians in accurately planning and describing surgical management of hemorrhoids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2573
retrieved_docs.content: Anal and rectal conditions are encompassed within the broader category of gastrointestinal disorder (اضْطِراب مَعِدِيّ مِعَوِيّ). As a subset of this wider gastrointestinal spectrum, anal and rectal conditions affect the distal portion of the digestive tract and share many pathophysiological mechanisms, diagnostic considerations, and therapeutic strategies with other gastrointestinal disorders. Recognizing that anal and rectal conditions have a broader relationship with gastrointestinal disorder encourages clinicians to adopt a comprehensive, system‑based approach to evaluation and management, ensuring that patient care addresses both localized anorectal pathology and its context within the entire gastrointestinal system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2657
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of the hemorrhoidal plexus is identified as the anatomical site where بواسير غير محددة من دون ذكر مضاعفات (unspecified hemorrhoids without mention of complications) are found, indicating that these hemorrhoidal lesions are located within the normal vascular network of the rectal and anal region in the absence of reported complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1075
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of hemorrhoidal plexus is the anatomical site where the clinical finding بواسير غير محددة من دون ذكر مضاعفات (unspecified hemorrhoids without mention of complications) is observed, and it also serves as the procedural site for عملية البواسير (hemorrhoid surgery). Hemorrhoid procedures represent the broader category of interventions that include عملية البواسير as a specific type of hemorrhoid surgery. This interconnection underscores the importance of the hemorrhoidal plexus anatomy in both diagnosing hemorrhoidal disease and guiding the appropriate surgical treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 715
retrieved_docs.content: The internal rectal venous plexus is identified as the anatomical site where the procedure known as ربط الباسور (hemorrhoid ligation) is performed. ربط الباسور is classified as عملية البواسير, a hemorrhoid operation, and this operation is a specific type of hemorrhoid procedures. Consequently, hemorrhoid procedures encompass عملية البواسير, including the ligation technique that targets the internal rectal venous plexus, underscoring the anatomical focus and categorical classification of these treatments for effective clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2607
retrieved_docs.content: The internal rectal venous plexus serves as the anatomical location where hemorrhoids are identified, and it is also the operative site for the procedure known as ربط الباسور. Recognizing this dual role of the internal rectal venous plexus is important for clinicians diagnosing hemorrhoidal disease and for surgeons performing ربط الباسور, ensuring that interventions are accurately targeted to the appropriate vascular structures within the rectum.

Overall, the knowledge emphasizes precise identification of anatomical sites such as the vaginal wall and awareness of complications like fistula to guide effective surgical management of pelvic floor disorders and related diverticula.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2169
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhoids encompass presentations that are described as بواسير غير محددة من دون ذكر مضاعفات, which translates to unspecified hemorrhoids without mention of complications. This indicates that cases of hemorrhoids that lack detailed specification or documented complications are still classified within the broader condition of hemorrhoids, guiding clinicians to consider appropriate evaluation and management even when the presentation is nonspecific.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2347
retrieved_docs.content: The closure of rectovaginal fistula by transanal approach, the repair of rectovaginal fistula, the repair of sigmoidovaginal fistula, the repair of vaginocutaneous fistula, and the repair of vaginoperineal fistula all share the same direct morphology, which is described by the term “النَّاسُور.” This common morphological characteristic indicates that, despite involving different anatomical sites, these fistula‑related procedures address a similar structural feature. Recognizing that “النَّاسُور” underlies the pathology of each of these fistulas can aid clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical techniques and in anticipating comparable intra‑operative considerations across these varied but morphologically related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2371
retrieved_docs.content: Repair of male urethrocutaneous fistula, repair of perineourethroscrotal fistula, repair of rectovaginal fistula, repair of urethroscrotal fistula, and repair of vaginoperineal fistula are surgical procedures that all share the same direct morphological characteristic, which is described by the term “النَّاسُور.” This common morphology suggests a unified anatomical or pathological feature across these different fistula repairs, potentially informing consistent diagnostic criteria and surgical approaches for these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 1141
retrieved_docs.content: The internal rectal venous plexus is the anatomical location where hemorrhoids are identified and where the procedure ربط الباسور is performed. Hemorrhoids are classified as a type of venous disorder (اضطراب الوريد) and are considered a form of بواسير غير محددة من دون ذكر مضاعفات (unspecified hemorrhoids without mention of complications). The Structure of hemorrhoidal plexus likewise serves as a finding site for بواسير غير محددة من دون ذكر مضاعفات and as a procedural site for عملية البواسير (hemorrhoid surgery). Hemorrhoid procedures are defined as عملية البواسير, and the specific intervention ربط الباسور is classified as a type of عملية البواسير. Together, these relationships delineate the anatomical basis of hemorrhoidal disease within the venous system and its management through defined surgical procedures.
